name: Jane Austen
description: Wit, romance, and razor-sharp social observation from the novels of England's most beloved chronicler of the human heart.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife.
quotes.notes: The novel's celebrated opening line, the narrator setting an ironic tone.

quotes.content: She is tolerable: but not handsome enough to tempt me.
quotes.notes: Mr. Darcy's slighting remark about Elizabeth Bennet at the Meryton assembly.

quotes.content: I could easily forgive his pride, if he had not mortified mine.
quotes.notes: Elizabeth Bennet, on her first impression of Mr. Darcy.

quotes.content: Vanity and pride are different things, though the words are often used synonymously.
quotes.notes: Mary Bennet, moralizing on the difference between the two faults.

quotes.content: I declare, after all, there is no enjoyment like reading!
quotes.notes: Caroline Bingley, affecting a love of books to impress Mr. Darcy.

quotes.content: I have been a selfish being all my life, in practice, though not in principle.
quotes.notes: Mr. Darcy, reflecting on his character to Elizabeth near the novel's end.

quotes.content: What are men to rocks and mountains?
quotes.notes: Elizabeth Bennet, delighting in the prospect of a tour to the Lakes.

quotes.content: A lady's imagination is very rapid; it jumps from admiration to love, from love to matrimony, in a moment.
quotes.notes: Mr. Darcy, teasing about how quickly affection is supposed.

quotes.content: Till this moment, I never knew myself.
quotes.notes: Elizabeth Bennet, after reading Darcy's letter and confronting her own prejudice.

quotes.content: For what do we live, but to make sport for our neighbours, and laugh at them in our turn?
quotes.notes: Mr. Bennet, in his characteristically dry humour.

quotes.content: In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.
quotes.notes: Mr. Darcy's first, ill-fated proposal to Elizabeth at Hunsford.

quotes.content: My good opinion once lost is lost for ever.
quotes.notes: Mr. Darcy, describing his unyielding temper to Elizabeth.

quotes.content: Know your own happiness. You want nothing but patience—or give it a more fascinating name, call it hope.
quotes.notes: Mrs. Dashwood, offering encouragement to Edward Ferrars.

quotes.content: The more I know of the world, the more am I convinced that I shall never see a man whom I can really love.
quotes.notes: Marianne Dashwood, declaring her romantic ideals to her mother.

quotes.content: I will be calm; I will be mistress of myself.
quotes.notes: Marianne Dashwood, steeling herself on seeing Willoughby in London.

quotes.content: It is not time or opportunity that is to determine intimacy;—it is disposition alone. Seven years would be insufficient to make some people acquainted with each other, and seven days are more than enough for others.
quotes.notes: Marianne Dashwood, on the swift growth of true affinity.

quotes.content: If I could but know his heart, everything would become easy.
quotes.notes: Elinor Dashwood, in a rare moment of betraying her feelings for Edward.

quotes.content: If I loved you less, I might be able to talk about it more.
quotes.notes: Mr. Knightley, declaring his love to Emma Woodhouse.

quotes.content: One half of the world cannot understand the pleasures of the other.
quotes.notes: Emma Woodhouse, to her father, Mr. Woodhouse.

quotes.content: There is no charm equal to tenderness of heart.
quotes.notes: Emma Woodhouse, to herself, in praise of her friend Harriet.

quotes.content: Better be without sense, than misapply it as you do.
quotes.notes: Mr. Knightley, chiding Emma for her matchmaking.

quotes.content: Silly things do cease to be silly if they are done by sensible people in an impudent way.
quotes.notes: Emma Woodhouse, moralizing to herself.

quotes.content: We have all a better guide in ourselves, if we would attend to it, than any other person can be.
quotes.notes: Fanny Price, to Edmund Bertram, on the conscience.

quotes.content: Let other pens dwell on guilt and misery.
quotes.notes: The narrator, opening the novel's final chapter.

quotes.content: A large income is the best recipe for happiness I ever heard of.
quotes.notes: Mary Crawford, on the comforts of wealth.

quotes.content: Selfishness must always be forgiven, you know, because there is no hope of a cure.
quotes.notes: Mary Crawford, half-joking about her own faults.

quotes.content: I was quiet, but I was not blind.
quotes.notes: Fanny Price, to Mary Crawford, on what she had observed.

quotes.content: The person, be it gentleman or lady, who has not pleasure in a good novel, must be intolerably stupid.
quotes.notes: The narrator, in a spirited defence of novel-reading.

quotes.content: There is nothing I would not do for those who are really my friends. I have no notion of loving people by halves; it is not my nature.
quotes.notes: Isabella Thorpe, whose conduct soon belies her words.

quotes.content: But history, real solemn history, I cannot be interested in.
quotes.notes: Catherine Morland, on her dislike of history books.

quotes.content: Friendship is certainly the finest balm for the pangs of disappointed love.
quotes.notes: The narrator, with characteristic irony.

quotes.content: You pierce my soul. I am half agony, half hope.
quotes.notes: Captain Wentworth, in the letter he writes to Anne Elliot.

quotes.content: I have loved none but you.
quotes.notes: Captain Wentworth, in his letter to Anne Elliot.

quotes.content: I can listen no longer in silence. I must speak to you by such means as are within my reach.
quotes.notes: The opening of Captain Wentworth's letter to Anne Elliot.

quotes.content: All the privilege I claim for my own sex (it is not a very enviable one; you need not covet it), is that of loving longest, when existence or when hope is gone.
quotes.notes: Anne Elliot, in her debate with Captain Harville about constancy.

quotes.content: Men have had every advantage of us in telling their own story.
quotes.notes: Anne Elliot, on why books slight women's constancy.

quotes.content: She had been forced into prudence in her youth, she learned romance as she grew older: the natural sequel of an unnatural beginning.
quotes.notes: The narrator, on Anne Elliot's changed view of love.
